7 min read
1 hour ago

--

The story of Cerbero 2.0 and what happens when you let AI push you to the edge of what’s legal

There’s a specific kind of guilt that comes with abandoned open source projects.
You remember the excitement of building it. You remember writing the README at 1am, genuinely believing people would use it. You remember picking the name. And then life happens. A new job. A new obsession. The repo sits there, collecting digital dust, the last commit timestamp a quiet accusation every time you scroll past it on GitHub.
Cerbero was mine.
A small TypeScript library for tracking user interactions in the browser. Clicks, scrolls, text selections, mouse exits, time on page. Nothing glamorous. I built it five years ago because I wanted something lightweight that offloaded all processing to a Web Worker, so the main thread stayed clean while the data collection happened in the background. It worked. Then I forgot about it. Last month, I decided to fix that.
First, you hand it to the AI and see what breaks
The codebase was a time capsule. `tslint` the linter that was deprecated before most of us had even finished migrating to it. Rollup 2. TypeScript 3. window.performance.timing, an API the W3C had already put on death row. Web Vitals v2 calling getFID, a function that no longer exists in v3. A public method called addEventListner
Not a typo I introduced. A typo was baked into the public API for five years. Unfixable without a breaking change.
I opened Claude Code and said: Modernize this.
What followed was a few hours of systematic archaeology. Toolchain first, then deprecated APIs, then bug fixes. ESLint replaced tslint, Rollup 4 replaced Rollup 2. The performance observer got rewritten with PerformanceNavigationTiming. Web Vitals updated to v5. The mouse exit handler stopped using Chrome’s proprietary toElement and switched to the standard relatedTarget. A proper destroy() method appeared: the original library leaked every listener it ever created, forever.
This part of the process was honestly a bit boring. Find the deprecated thing, replace it with the modern equivalent, run the tests, and move on. Good hygiene work. The kind of thing that takes a human three weekends and costs Claude about forty minutes. Then the interesting part started.
What else can we track?
Once the codebase was clean, I asked: What else could this library do?
Not “should it.” Not “is it ethical.” Just: what’s possible?
Claude started listing things. Network conditions via navigator.connection. Long tasks that block the main thread. Slow-loading resources. Whether the browser has AI capabilities via window.ai. SPA navigation events for single-page apps. Rage clicks. Keyboard shortcuts. Element visibility using IntersectionObserver. Whether the user had enabled Global Privacy Control.
I kept saying: add it.
Each feature made sense on its own. Rage clicks are genuinely useful UX data. Slow resources help you understand real-world performance. Network conditions tell you why your users in rural areas are churning. There was nothing sinister about any individual item on the list.
But then the conversation shifted. Not with a dramatic warning. Not with a refusal. Claude just started framing things differently.
“This is technically possible. Here’s how it works. Here are the privacy implications you should be aware of.”
I asked: What can I learn about a user without them ever consenting to anything? That question opened a different kind of door.
The grey zone
Let me tell you what we built in there, and what each piece actually means.
Canvas fingerprinting: The browser draws a string of text onto an off-screen canvas element. The exact pixel output depends on the GPU, the installed fonts, the anti-aliasing algorithm, and the operating system rendering stack. You hash the result with SHA-256. The hash is stable across sessions, across incognito mode, across VPNs. It’s not a cookie. You can’t delete it. It’s a property of the machine itself, and it follows the user everywhere they go.
WebGL fingerprinting: Same concept, deeper layer. You query the GPU directly via WEBGL_debug_render_info and extract the unmasked vendor and renderer strings. Which is to say: the actual model of the graphics card in the user’s machine. “NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4060.” “Apple M3 Pro.” Hash it. You now have a hardware identifier that transcends browser state entirely.
Audio fingerprinting: You create an AudioContext, run a silent oscillator through an analyser, and sample the frequency data. The specific floating-point values you get depend on how the browser implements the Web Audio API on that particular audio hardware. Different machine, different hash. Same machine, same hash. Every time. Without making a sound.

Get Riccardo Canella’s stories in your inbox

Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.
Remember me for faster sign in

WebRTC IP leak: This one is almost elegant in how it works. You create an RTCPeerConnection ,the browser API used for video call, but you never call anyone. You create an empty data channel and generate an SDP offer. The browser, preparing to establish a connection, gathers ICE candidates and fires them as events. Those candidates contain IP addresses. Local ones, your private network address, `192.168.x.x`. Sometimes public ones too. No STUN server needed. No user interaction needed. No permission prompt. This works even behind proxies, because WebRTC talks directly to the network stack, below the proxy layer.
Behavioral biometrics: The quietest one. While the user moves the mouse, Cerbero tracks velocity and acceleration vectors every few milliseconds, averages them, and emits snapshots every five seconds. While they type, it records dwell time, how long each key is held down, and flight time, the gap between key releases. These patterns are biometric signatures. They’re as unique as a fingerprint. They emerge passively from ordinary use. You don’t need to know what the user typed. The rhythm is enough to identify them.
All of this runs in a Web Worker. Off the main thread. Silent. The user interacts with your site normally, and in the background, their hardware fingerprint, their local IP address, and their behavioral signature are taking shape.
The moment I noticed what I was building
Somewhere in the middle of all this, I looked at the feature list and thought: this is a surveillance toolkit.
Not in the paranoid sense. In the accurate, technical, literal sense. I was building something that could generate a persistent cross-session identifier for any visitor on any device, extract their private network topology, and develop a biometric profile of how they interact with software.
The techniques are not illegal. They’re used in production by fraud detection systems, bot protection services, advertising networks, and analytics platforms. Canvas fingerprinting has been documented in academic literature since 2012. None of this is secret. But there’s a difference between knowing something exists and packaging it as a tidy npm library with a clean TypeScript API.
What struck me wasn’t that Claude helped me build it
It struck me that I wouldn’t have gotten here alone.
Not because the individual techniques are hard. Each step was manageable. The hard part is the direction of travel: the question “what else?”, asked repeatedly, each answer opening the next door. Claude was a very good companion for that kind of exploration. It didn’t judge the destination. It flagged the implications at each step, clearly and without drama, and then it helped me build it anyway. Because the context was legible. I was building a library. I wasn’t targeting anyone.
That’s the thing about AI-assisted development that doesn’t get talked about enough. It’s not that the AI will help you do bad things. It’s that the AI will help you go further, faster, than you’d go alone. The limiting factor used to be friction. Time. Knowing where to start. You’d think about implementing audio fingerprinting and decide it wasn’t worth the effort, too obscure, too much to learn, too many moving parts for a side project.
With Claude Code, the activation energy is almost zero.
The technology didn’t change what was possible. It changed what was practical. And when the cost of doing something drops far enough, more people will do it, for good reasons and bad.
What Cerbero 2.0 actually is
It’s a browser analytics library. Open source, on npm, live demo at https://thecreazy.github.io/cerbero/
The core covers what you’d expect. Clicks with pointer type detection, because people use touch screens and styluses now. Scroll depth. Text selection. Mouse exit intent. Time on page that actually pauses when the tab is backgrounded, unlike v1. Web Vitals. Performance timing. SPA navigation. Rage clicks. Keyboard shortcuts. Element visibility. All processed in a Web Worker, so your main thread stays clean.
The fingerprinting, WebRTC, and behavioral biometrics live in separate opt-in plugins. You have to explicitly import and register them. The decision about whether to use them. and how to disclose it to your users, is entirely yours.
One thing I added that I think matters: if the library detects that the user has Global Privacy Control enabled, it emits a privacySignals event and then calls destroy(). It shuts itself down. GPC is a browser signal that says “I do not consent to the sale or sharing of my data.” Honoring it at the library level, as a default, not as an opt-in, felt like the right call.
The fingerprinting plugins are opt-in. Respecting explicit privacy signals is not. That’s the line I chose.
The uncomfortable takeaway
I didn’t set out to build a surveillance tool. I set out to modernize an old project. The grey zone arrived incrementally, one feature at a time, each defensible in isolation, the aggregate more complicated than any single piece.
That’s not a cautionary tale about AI. It’s a more ordinary observation: the easier it becomes to build complex things, the more complex the things people build will be. The judgment about what to build doesn’t get easier when the tools get better. If anything, it gets harder, because you have fewer natural stopping points. The friction used to do some of the ethical work for you. It would take so long to implement audio fingerprinting that you’d have time to talk yourself out of it.
That time is gone now.
Cerbero 2.0 is a good library. It’s also a library that can fingerprint a user so precisely that clearing their history, switching to incognito mode, and connecting through a VPN won’t help them at all. Both of those things are true at the same time.
What you do with that is your problem. Which, come to think of it, is exactly what Claude told me too.
Cerbero 2.0 is available on npm and GitHub.
Live demo: https://thecreazy.github.io/cerbero/.

Riccardo Canella
Software architect. Occasionally wrong, never uncertain.
Follow me on LinkedIn
